questiontext.text: What does Forward Kinematics compute for a robotic manipulator?

generalfeedback.text: FK maps joint space → task space: given $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots)$ it returns the end-effector pose $(x, y, \phi)$.

answer.text: The position and orientation of the end-effector given joint angles

feedback.text: ✓ Correct. FK solves the mapping joint space → task space.

answer.text: The joint angles required to reach a target end-effector position

feedback.text: That is Inverse Kinematics (IK) — the reverse problem.

answer.text: The joint torques needed to move the robot

feedback.text: That is dynamics / inverse dynamics, not kinematics.
Velocity requires the Jacobian — a separate step beyond basic FK.

questiontext.text: For a 2R planar robot with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$, which expression gives the x-coordinate of the end-effector?

answer.text: x = l₁ cos θ₁ + l₂ cos(θ₁ + θ₂)

feedback.text: ✓ Correct — each link contributes its projection onto the x-axis.

answer.text: x = l₁ sin θ₁ + l₂ sin(θ₁ + θ₂)

feedback.text: That formula gives the y-coordinate, not x.

answer.text: x = l₁ cos θ₁ · l₂ cos θ₂

feedback.text: Link contributions are added, not multiplied, and the second angle is cumulative.
This would only be correct if both links were always parallel.

questiontext.text: How many Inverse Kinematics solutions can a 2R planar robot have for a given reachable target?

generalfeedback.text: A 2R arm typically has 2 solutions: elbow-up and elbow-down. At the boundary of the workspace there is 1, and outside it there are 0.

answer.text: 0, 1, or 2 depending on the target position

feedback.text: ✓ Correct — 2 (elbow-up/down) inside the workspace, 1 at the boundary, 0 outside.
Not true — most reachable targets have 2 solutions (elbow-up and elbow-down).
At the workspace boundary there is only 1 solution; outside there are none.
Infinitely many solutions arise in redundant robots (≥ 3 DOF for planar tasks).

questiontext.text: True or False: The reachable workspace of a 2R planar robot is always a solid disc with no holes.

generalfeedback.text: When $l_1 \neq l_2$ the inner boundary is a circle of radius $|l_1 - l_2|$, creating an annular (ring-shaped) workspace.

feedback.text: False — when the links have different lengths there is a hole of radius |l₁ − l₂| at the centre.
✓ Correct. When l₁ ≠ l₂ the inner circle |l₁ − l₂| is unreachable, forming an annulus.

answer.text: Joint angles → end-effector position (same as FK)

feedback.text: FK maps angles to position; the Jacobian relates velocities.
That is the transpose Jacobian relation $\tau = J^T F$, not the Jacobian itself.
That is the dynamics equation (Newton–Euler / Euler–Lagrange), not kinematics.

questiontext.text: A 2R planar robot has link lengths $l_1 = 1\,\text{m}$ and $l_2 = 0.8\,\text{m}$. What is its maximum reach (in metres)?
Match each term to its correct definition.

answer.text: Maps joint velocities to end-effector velocities
Configuration where the Jacobian loses rank
